records.ety: [Formerly a noun; e. g., “I trow it was in the dismalle.” Chaucer. Of uncertain origin; but perh. (as suggested by Skeat) from OF. disme, F. dme, tithe, the phrase dismal day properly meaning, the day when tithes must be paid. See Dime.]

quotes: An ugly fiend more foul than dismal day.

defs: Gloomy to the eye or ear; sorrowful and depressing to the feelings; foreboding; cheerless; dull; dreary; as, a dismal outlook; dismal stories; a dismal place.
quotes: Full well the busy whisper, circling round, Convey'd the dismal tidings when he frowned.
A dismal description of an English November.
